meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) electric potential, potential energy, and capacitance items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 19 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by direct calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). Figure-dependent items (specific equipotential diagrams, plate-geometry figures) and free-response/numeric items were skipped. Calculations use k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C², e = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² F/m.

questions.stem: An electron is placed in an electric field of 12.0 N/C directed to the right. What is the resulting force on the electron?
questions.explanation: The force magnitude is F = qE = (1.60×10⁻¹⁹ C)(12.0 N/C) = 1.92×10⁻¹⁸ N. Because the electron is negative, the force points opposite to the field, i.e., to the left.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (F = qE, opposite to field for a negative charge).

hints.prompt: What determines the electric force on a charged particle placed in a uniform electric field?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The magnitude is F = qE, and for a negative charge the force points opposite to the field.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The magnitude is F = q/E, and the force always points along the field.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The magnitude is F = E/q, and it is opposite the field only for positive charges.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is zero unless the charge is moving.

hints.rationale: F = qE gives the magnitude, and a negative charge is pushed opposite to the field direction.

hints.prompt: To get the magnitude of the force on this electron in the 12.0 N/C field, which calculation should you perform?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiply the elementary charge 1.60×10⁻¹⁹ C by the field 12.0 N/C.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiply 12.0 N/C by the electron's mass.

hints.rationale: F = qE means multiplying the charge magnitude by the field strength.

hints.prompt: The field points to the right. Which statement about the force direction is correct and lets you rule out answer choices?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the electron is negative, the force is opposite the field, so any 'to the right' option can be eliminated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the electron is negative, the force is along the field, so 'to the left' options can be eliminated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force direction cannot be determined from the given information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is perpendicular to the field, so both 'left' and 'right' options are wrong.

hints.rationale: A negative charge feels a force opposite the field, ruling out the two rightward options.

questions.stem: A -5.0 C charge is being forced south by a 60 N force. What are the magnitude and direction of the local electric field?
questions.explanation: The field magnitude is E = F/|q| = 60 N / 5.0 C = 12 N/C. For a negative charge the force is opposite to the field, so a southward force means the field points north.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (E = F/q; field opposite to force for negative charge).

hints.prompt: For a charge in an electric field, how are field, force, and charge related, including direction for a negative charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: E = F/|q|, and for a negative charge the field points opposite to the force.

choices.label: B
choices.text: E = F·q, and field and force always point the same way.

choices.label: C
choices.text: E = q/F, and the field opposes the force only for positive charges.

choices.label: D
choices.text: E = F/|q|, and the field always points the same direction as the force.

hints.rationale: The field magnitude is force per unit charge, and for a negative charge the field opposes the force.

hints.prompt: Which computation gives the magnitude of the field for this 60 N force on the 5.0 C charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Divide the 60 N force by the charge magnitude 5.0 C.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiply 60 N by 5.0 C and by k.

hints.rationale: E = F/|q| = 60 N ÷ 5.0 C gives the field magnitude.

hints.prompt: The force on the charge is toward the south. What does the charge's negative sign tell you about the field direction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The field points opposite the force (north), so the 'south' options can be eliminated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The field points along the force (south), so the 'north' options can be eliminated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sign does not affect direction; field and force align.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The field is perpendicular to the force.

hints.rationale: For a negative charge the field opposes the force, ruling out the two southward options.

questions.stem: How many electrons have to be moved by a car battery containing 7.20×10⁵ J at 12 V to reduce the energy by 1%?
questions.explanation: One percent of the energy is 0.01 × 7.20×10⁵ = 7.20×10³ J. Each electron moved through 12 V transfers qV = (1.60×10⁻¹⁹)(12) = 1.92×10⁻¹⁸ J. So the number of electrons is 7.20×10³ / 1.92×10⁻¹⁸ ≈ 3.75×10²¹.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (N = energy / (qV)).

hints.prompt: When a charge q moves through a potential difference V, how much energy does it transfer, and how do you count charges needed for a total energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each charge transfers qV; the number needed is (total energy)/(qV).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each charge transfers q/V; the number needed is (total energy)×qV.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each charge transfers V/q; the number needed is (total energy)×V.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Each charge transfers qV²; the number needed is (total energy)/V.

hints.rationale: Energy per charge is qV, so the count is the total energy divided by qV.

hints.prompt: What is the first quantity you must compute before dividing by the per-electron energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One percent of the stored energy, i.e., 0.01 × 7.20×10⁵ J.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The product of 7.20×10⁵ J and 12 V.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ninety-nine percent of the stored energy.

hints.rationale: Only the 1% being removed matters, so use 0.01 × the total energy.

hints.prompt: Dividing a few thousand joules by the tiny per-electron energy gives a very large—but not astronomically large—number. Which two options can be ruled out as far too large?
hints.rationale: 7.2×10³ J divided by ~10⁻¹⁸ J per electron is far below 10²⁶, so the two largest options are impossible.

questions.stem: A typical AAA battery can move 2000 C of charge at 1.5 V. How long will this run a 50 mW LED?
questions.explanation: Energy stored = QV = 2000 C × 1.5 V = 3000 J. Run time = energy / power = 3000 J / 0.050 W = 60,000 s = 1000 minutes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (E = QV; t = E/P = 60,000 s = 1000 min).

hints.prompt: How do you find the energy delivered by moving charge through a voltage, then the time it can power a device?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Energy = QV, and time = energy / power.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Energy = Q/V, and time = energy × power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Energy = QV, and time = power / energy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Energy = V/Q, and time = energy / power.

hints.rationale: Stored energy is charge times voltage, and run time is energy divided by power.

hints.prompt: Which setup gives the run time (watch the units of power)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compute (2000 C × 1.5 V), then divide by 0.050 W.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compute (2000 C × 1.5 V), then divide by 50 W.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compute (2000 C ÷ 1.5 V), then multiply by 0.050 W.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compute (2000 C × 1.5 V), then multiply by 0.050 W.

hints.rationale: Energy QV is divided by the power expressed in watts (50 mW = 0.050 W).

hints.prompt: What conversion is essential to avoid a factor-of-1000 error in the run time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Convert 50 mW to 0.050 W before dividing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Convert the final answer to milliseconds.

hints.rationale: Using milliwatts instead of watts changes the run time by a factor of 1000.

questions.stem: What is the electric potential energy of a system consisting of two point charges, one +2.0 µC and the other -3.0 µC, placed 1.2 m away from each other?
questions.explanation: U = kq₁q₂/r = (8.99×10⁹)(2.0×10⁻⁶)(-3.0×10⁻⁶)/(1.2) = (8.99×10⁹)(-6.0×10⁻¹²)/1.2 ≈ -4.5×10⁻² J. The negative sign reflects the attractive (opposite-charge) interaction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (U = kq₁q₂/r).

hints.prompt: What formula gives the electric potential energy of a pair of point charges, and what sign results for opposite charges?

choices.label: A
choices.text: U = kq₁q₂/r; opposite charges give a negative U.

choices.label: B
choices.text: U = kq₁q₂/r²; opposite charges give a positive U.

choices.label: C
choices.text: U = kq₁q₂/r; opposite charges give a positive U.

choices.label: D
choices.text: U = kq₁/(q₂r); the sign is always positive.

hints.rationale: Pair potential energy varies as 1/r and is negative when the charges are opposite.

hints.prompt: Which substitution correctly sets up U for a +2.0 µC and a −3.0 µC charge 1.2 m apart?
hints.rationale: Multiply the two signed charges and divide by r (not r²), keeping the negative sign.

hints.prompt: What must be true about the sign of the answer for these two charges?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must be negative because the charges are opposite (attractive), ruling out the positive options.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must be positive because energy is always positive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It must be negative because both charges are negative.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sign cannot be determined without more information.

hints.rationale: Opposite charges give negative potential energy, eliminating both positive-valued options.

questions.stem: A system has +2.00 µC charges at (50 cm, 0) and (-50 cm, 0) and a -1.00 µC charge at (0, 70 cm). As the y-coordinate of the -1.00 µC charge increases, the potential energy ____. As the y-coordinate of the -1.00 µC charge decreases, the potential energy ____.
questions.explanation: The -1.00 µC charge is attracted to the two +2.00 µC charges, so the pair-wise potential energy is negative. Moving the negative charge farther away (y increases) makes the energy less negative—i.e., it increases (toward zero). Moving it closer (y decreases) makes the energy more negative—i.e., it decreases.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (attractive U is negative; |U| grows as separation shrinks).

hints.prompt: For opposite (attractive) charges, how does their potential energy change as they move apart or closer together?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moving apart makes U less negative (increases toward zero); moving closer makes U more negative (decreases).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Moving apart makes U more negative; moving closer makes U less negative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both moving apart and moving closer increase U.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Distance does not affect U for attractive charges.

hints.rationale: Attractive potential energy is negative and rises toward zero as separation grows.

hints.prompt: The −1.00 µC charge is attracted to the two +2.00 µC charges near the x-axis. As its y-coordinate increases it moves ___ them; as y decreases it moves ___ them.
hints.rationale: The positive charges sit near y = 0, so raising y moves the negative charge away and lowering y brings it closer.

hints.prompt: As the negative charge moves farther from the positive charges (y increases), what happens to the potential energy, and which options does that eliminate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases (becomes less negative), eliminating the two 'decreases, …' options.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It decreases (becomes more negative), eliminating the two 'increases, …' options.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It stays constant, eliminating all but 'no change' options.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It doubles, so no options can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: Greater separation makes the attractive energy less negative, ruling out the two options that start with 'decreases'.

questions.stem: Take a square configuration of point charges—two positive and two negative, all of the same magnitude, with like charges sharing diagonals. What will happen to the internal (potential) energy of this system if one of the negative charges becomes a positive charge of the same magnitude?
questions.explanation: The original alternating arrangement (adjacent charges opposite) is a low-energy configuration dominated by attraction. Converting one negative charge to positive creates three like (positive) charges with strong mutual repulsion, raising the system's potential energy.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (more like-charge repulsion raises potential energy).

hints.prompt: In a system of point charges, how does creating more like-charge pairs affect the total potential energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: More like-charge (repulsive) pairs raise the potential energy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: More like-charge pairs lower the potential energy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Changing a charge's sign never affects the potential energy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Like-charge pairs contribute negative potential energy.

hints.rationale: Repulsion between like charges stores positive energy, raising the total.

hints.prompt: Originally adjacent charges are opposite (attractive). After one negative charge becomes positive, the system has ___ compared with before.

choices.label: A
choices.text: three positive charges that repel one another, adding positive energy

choices.label: B
choices.text: all opposite-neighbor pairs preserved, so no new repulsion

choices.label: D
choices.text: a net zero charge that cancels all energy

hints.rationale: Flipping one sign yields three like charges whose mutual repulsion adds energy.

hints.prompt: Because flipping the sign definitely changes which pairs attract or repel, which answer choices can be ruled out immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'No change' and 'not enough information' can be ruled out.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be ruled out.

hints.rationale: The energy clearly changes and is determinable, so the 'no change' and 'not enough information' options are out.

questions.stem: A system has +2.00 µC charges at (50 cm, 0) and (-50 cm, 0) and a -1.00 µC charge at (0, 70 cm) moving with a velocity in the -y-direction. When the -1.00 µC charge reaches (0, 0), the potential energy is at a ____ and the kinetic energy is at a ____.
questions.explanation: At (0, 0) the negative charge is closest to both positive charges, so the attractive potential energy is most negative (a minimum). By conservation of energy, the kinetic energy is correspondingly at a maximum.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (closest approach ⇒ minimum PE, maximum KE).

hints.prompt: For a charge attracted to fixed charges, where is its potential energy lowest, and what does energy conservation say about kinetic energy there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potential energy is lowest at the closest approach, and kinetic energy is largest there.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potential energy is highest at closest approach, and kinetic energy is lowest there.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potential and kinetic energy both peak at closest approach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kinetic energy stays constant regardless of position.

hints.rationale: Deepest (most negative) potential energy occurs closest in, so kinetic energy is greatest by conservation of energy.

hints.prompt: At (0, 0), the −1.00 µC charge is ___ the two positive charges compared with any other point on its path.
hints.rationale: The origin lies nearest both +2.00 µC charges, the point of closest approach.

hints.prompt: Since mechanical energy is conserved, the potential- and kinetic-energy extremes must be opposite. Which two options can be eliminated because they pair the same type of extreme?
hints.rationale: When one energy is a maximum the other must be a minimum, so the matching-extreme pairs are impossible.

questions.stem: Two plates lie horizontally, stacked with one 10.0 cm above the other. The upper plate is held at +100 V. What is the magnitude and direction of the electric field between the plates if the lower plate is held at +50.0 V, and if it is held at -50.0 V?
questions.explanation: E = ΔV/d. With the lower plate at +50 V: ΔV = 100 - 50 = 50 V over 0.100 m → 500 V/m. With the lower plate at -50 V: ΔV = 100 - (-50) = 150 V over 0.100 m → 1500 V/m. The field points from high to low potential, i.e., downward from the +100 V upper plate.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (E = ΔV/d; field points high-to-low potential).

hints.prompt: How is the uniform field between two parallel plates related to their potential difference and separation, and which way does it point?

choices.label: A
choices.text: E = ΔV/d, directed from the higher-potential plate to the lower.

choices.label: B
choices.text: E = ΔV·d, directed from low to high potential.

choices.label: C
choices.text: E = d/ΔV, directed from high to low potential.

choices.label: D
choices.text: E = ΔV/d, directed from low to high potential.

hints.rationale: The field equals potential difference over distance and points from high to low potential.

hints.prompt: For the two cases (lower plate at +50 V, then −50 V), which potential differences over 0.100 m should you use?
hints.rationale: Use the potential difference across the gap for each case: 50 V and 150 V over 0.100 m.

hints.prompt: The upper plate is at the higher potential (+100 V). Which way does the field point, and what does that eliminate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Downward (high to low), eliminating the 'directed upward' options.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Upward (low to high), eliminating the 'directed downward' options.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The direction depends only on the lower plate's sign.

hints.rationale: The field runs from the higher (+100 V) upper plate downward, ruling out both 'upward' options.

questions.stem: Three parallel conducting plates are spaced 3.0 cm apart from one another. The leftmost is at a potential of +45 V, the middle one is held at ground (0 V), and the rightmost is at -75 V. What is the magnitude of the average electric field experienced by an electron traveling across from the left plate to the right plate? (Assume the middle plate has holes for the electron to pass through.)
questions.explanation: The total potential difference from the left plate (+45 V) to the right plate (-75 V) is 45 - (-75) = 120 V, over a total distance of 3.0 + 3.0 = 6.0 cm = 0.060 m. The average field is 120 V / 0.060 m = 2000 V/m.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (average E = total ΔV / total distance).

hints.prompt: How do you find the average electric field between two points at different potentials?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Divide the total potential difference between the endpoints by the total distance between them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiply the total potential difference by the total distance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use only the middle plate's potential.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Average the two end potentials, then multiply by distance.

hints.rationale: Average field is the end-to-end potential difference divided by the end-to-end distance.

hints.prompt: Which total potential difference and distance apply from the left plate to the right plate?
hints.rationale: The endpoints are +45 V and −75 V, separated by the full 6.0 cm.

hints.prompt: Why is it a mistake to base the average field on the middle (0 V) plate or a single 3.0 cm gap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The average over the whole path uses the end-to-end potential difference and full distance, not an intermediate plate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The middle plate carries all the current.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The electron stops at the middle plate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the middle plate's potential matters for the average.

hints.rationale: The average field spans the entire trajectory, so the intermediate plate and a single gap are the wrong basis.

questions.stem: A certain electric-potential isoline graph has isolines every 5.0 V. If six of these lines cross a 40 cm path drawn between two points of interest, what is the magnitude of the average electric field along this path?
questions.explanation: Crossing six 5.0-V isolines corresponds to a potential change of 6 × 5.0 = 30 V over the 0.40 m path. The average field magnitude is E = ΔV/d = 30 V / 0.40 m = 75 V/m.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (ΔV = 30 V over 0.40 m).

hints.prompt: How is average electric field related to the number of equally spaced equipotential lines crossed and the path length?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiply the number of lines by the spacing to get ΔV, then divide by the path length.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Divide the number of lines by the spacing, then multiply by the path length.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiply the spacing by the path length.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Divide the path length by the number of lines.

hints.rationale: Total ΔV is lines × spacing, and the average field is that divided by the distance.

hints.prompt: What potential difference corresponds to crossing six 5.0-V isolines, and over what distance?
hints.rationale: Six 5.0-V steps give the total potential change across the 0.40 m path.

hints.prompt: Which unit conversion must you get right to avoid an order-of-magnitude error?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Converting 40 cm to 0.40 m before dividing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Converting the final answer to volts.

hints.rationale: Leaving the path length in centimetres would shift the field by a factor of 100.

questions.stem: As isolines of electric potential get closer together, the electric field gets stronger. By analogy, what shape would a hill have where the isolines of gravitational potential (contour lines) get closer together?
questions.explanation: Closely spaced contour lines indicate a rapid change in elevation over a short horizontal distance—that is, a steeper slope. This mirrors how closely spaced equipotentials mean a stronger electric field.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (close contours ⇒ steep gradient).

hints.prompt: By analogy between electric equipotentials and gravitational contour lines, what does closer spacing of the lines indicate about the gradient (steepness)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Closer spacing means a larger gradient — a steeper slope.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Closer spacing means a smaller gradient — a shallower slope.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spacing has no relation to steepness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Closer spacing means a constant slope.

hints.rationale: A larger change per distance (closely spaced lines) is a steeper gradient, mirroring a stronger field.

hints.prompt: Closely spaced contour lines mean the elevation changes ___ over a given horizontal distance.
hints.rationale: Tightly packed contours indicate a large elevation change over a short horizontal run.

hints.prompt: Which answer choices describe things unrelated to how closely spaced the contour lines are, and can be ruled out?
hints.rationale: Spacing changes describe how the slope varies, so 'constant slope' and 'U-shape' are irrelevant here.

questions.stem: How much work is necessary to keep a positive point charge in a circular orbit around a negative point charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A lot; this system is unstable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Just a little; the isolines are far enough apart that crossing them doesn't take much work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: None; the charge travels along an isoline (equipotential), which requires no work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There's not enough information to tell.

questions.explanation: A circular orbit at fixed radius stays on a single equipotential surface (constant electric potential). Moving along an equipotential involves no change in potential energy, so no work is required.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (circular orbit = constant-radius equipotential ⇒ zero work).

hints.prompt: How much work is done moving a charge along a path of constant electric potential (an equipotential)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero, because there is no change in potential energy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A large amount, because the field is strong.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It depends on the charge's speed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Work equals the charge times the potential.

hints.rationale: No potential change along an equipotential means zero work.

hints.prompt: A circular orbit at fixed radius around the central charge stays on ___.
hints.rationale: At constant radius the potential is constant, so the path is one equipotential.

hints.prompt: Since the radius (and thus the potential) stays constant, which answer choices can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Constant potential means the work is small/none and fully determinable, ruling out the 'a lot' and 'not enough information' options.

questions.stem: Two parallel-plate capacitors are otherwise identical, except the second one has twice the plate separation of the first. If placed in otherwise identical circuits (same voltage), how much charge will the second capacitor hold compared to the first?
questions.explanation: Capacitance is C = ε₀A/d, so doubling the separation d halves the capacitance. At the same voltage, Q = CV, so the stored charge is also halved.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (C = ε₀A/d; Q = CV at fixed V).

hints.prompt: How does a parallel-plate capacitor's capacitance depend on plate separation, and how does stored charge depend on capacitance at fixed voltage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: C is inversely proportional to separation; at fixed V, Q ∝ C.

choices.label: B
choices.text: C is directly proportional to separation; at fixed V, Q ∝ C.

choices.label: C
choices.text: C is inversely proportional to separation; Q is independent of C.

choices.label: D
choices.text: C does not depend on separation.

hints.rationale: Since C = ε₀A/d and Q = CV, larger separation lowers C and thus the charge at fixed voltage.

hints.prompt: Doubling the plate separation changes the capacitance to ___, so at the same voltage the charge changes in the same proportion.
hints.rationale: C ∝ 1/d, so doubling d halves the capacitance.

hints.prompt: Because larger separation lowers capacitance, the stored charge must ___, ruling out which options?

choices.label: A
choices.text: decrease, ruling out 'four times as much' and 'twice as much'

choices.label: B
choices.text: increase, ruling out 'the same' and 'half as much'

choices.label: C
choices.text: stay the same, ruling out all changing options

choices.label: D
choices.text: increase, ruling out 'half as much' only

hints.rationale: Lower capacitance means less charge, so the two 'more charge' options are impossible.

questions.stem: A parallel-plate capacitor with adjustable-size square plates is placed in a circuit with a fixed voltage. How does the charge on the capacitor change as the length of the sides of the (square) plates is increased?
questions.explanation: For square plates of side L, the area is A = L². Since C = ε₀A/d and Q = CV at fixed voltage, Q ∝ A = L². So the charge grows in proportion to the square of the side length.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (Q ∝ A = L²).

hints.prompt: For square capacitor plates of side length L at fixed voltage, how does stored charge depend on L?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Q ∝ area = L², so it grows with the square of the side length.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Q is independent of plate size.

hints.rationale: Because C ∝ A and Q = CV, and the square plate's area is L², charge grows as L².

hints.prompt: For square plates, the area in C = ε₀A/d is expressed in terms of side length L as ___.
hints.rationale: A square of side L has area L².

hints.prompt: Increasing the plate size increases the area, so the charge must ___, ruling out which options?

choices.label: A
choices.text: grow, ruling out the two 'shrinks' options

choices.label: B
choices.text: shrink, ruling out the two 'grows' options

choices.label: C
choices.text: stay constant, ruling out all options

hints.rationale: More area means more capacitance and charge, so both 'shrinks' options are out.

questions.stem: A student varied one dimension of a square parallel-plate capacitor (in a circuit with a battery) and measured the resulting charge, obtaining: dimension 1.00 → 0.50 µC, 1.10 → 0.61 µC, 1.20 → 0.71 µC, 1.30 → 0.86 µC. Which dimension was being varied?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both the area and the length of a side

questions.explanation: The charge rises as the dimension increases (so it is not the plate separation, for which Q ∝ 1/d would decrease). Checking the ratio: Q(1.30)/Q(1.00) = 0.86/0.50 ≈ 1.72, close to (1.30)² = 1.69, so Q ∝ (dimension)². That quadratic dependence matches varying the side length L, because area = L².
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (data table small and fully in text). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (Q ∝ dimension² ⇒ side length, since A = L²).

hints.prompt: If a capacitor's stored charge increases as the square of a varied dimension, which relationship is implied?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Charge is proportional to the square of that dimension.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Charge is inversely proportional to that dimension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Charge is independent of that dimension.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charge is proportional to the dimension itself (linear).

hints.rationale: A squared growth in charge signals a quadratic dependence on the varied dimension.

hints.prompt: The data show charge increasing as the dimension increases. What does that rule out right away?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The plate separation, since larger separation would decrease the charge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The side length, since it has no effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The area, since area does not affect charge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing can be ruled out from the trend.

hints.rationale: Charge falls with separation (Q ∝ 1/d), so a rising trend excludes separation.

hints.prompt: The charge roughly quadruples as the dimension is scaled up, a squared dependence. Which choice is a trap because the two dimensions are not varied independently here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Both the area and the length of a side' is a trap, since only one dimension is varied at a time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The area' is a trap because area never affects charge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The length of a side' is physically impossible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'The distance between the plates' fits the increasing trend.

hints.rationale: The student varied only one dimension, so the 'both' option is a distractor.

questions.stem: What happens to the energy stored in a circuit (at fixed battery voltage) as you increase the number of capacitors connected in parallel? In series?
questions.explanation: Adding capacitors in parallel increases the total capacitance, raising the stored energy (½CV² at fixed V). Adding capacitors in series decreases the total capacitance, lowering the stored energy.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (parallel raises C; series lowers C; U = ½CV²).

hints.prompt: At fixed voltage, how does total capacitance change when capacitors are added in parallel versus in series, and how does stored energy follow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parallel increases total C; series decreases it; stored energy (½CV²) follows C.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parallel decreases total C; series increases it; energy follows C.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both parallel and series increase total C.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capacitor arrangement does not affect stored energy.

hints.rationale: Parallel adds capacitance and series reduces it, and energy tracks capacitance at fixed voltage.

hints.prompt: Adding more capacitors in parallel makes the total capacitance ___, while adding more in series makes it ___.
hints.rationale: Parallel capacitances add; series capacitances combine to less than the smallest.

hints.prompt: Since energy is ½CV² at fixed V, adding capacitors in parallel (which raises C) makes stored energy ___, eliminating which options?
hints.rationale: Parallel raises capacitance and energy, ruling out both options whose first blank is 'decreases'.

questions.stem: Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with no dielectric, square plates 1.00 m on a side, separated by 1.00 mm. What is the energy stored in this capacitor with 3.00×10³ V applied to it? (ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² F/m.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (C = ε₀A/d; U = ½CV²).

hints.prompt: Which two formulas give the energy stored in a parallel-plate capacitor from its geometry and applied voltage?
hints.rationale: Capacitance is ε₀A/d and the stored energy is one-half C times voltage squared.

hints.prompt: Which substitution sets up the capacitance for this capacitor (A = 1.00 m², d = 1.00 mm)?
hints.rationale: Convert 1.00 mm to 1.00×10⁻³ m and divide the area by it.

hints.prompt: A tabletop capacitor at a few kilovolts stores only a small amount of energy. Which two options are physically unreasonable (far too large)?
hints.rationale: A nanofarad-scale capacitor cannot store 10¹¹–10¹⁴ J, so those two options are impossible.

questions.stem: For a parallel-plate capacitor connected to a fixed voltage, if the plates grow in area (separation unchanged), the capacitance ____ and the stored energy ____.
questions.explanation: Capacitance C = ε₀A/d increases with area. At fixed voltage, the stored energy U = ½CV² increases in proportion to C. So both the capacitance and the stored energy increase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 19 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (C ∝ A; U = ½CV² at fixed V).

hints.prompt: At fixed voltage, how do capacitance and stored energy respond when plate area increases?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both increase, since C ∝ A and U = ½CV².

hints.rationale: Larger area raises capacitance, and energy rises with capacitance at fixed voltage.

hints.prompt: Increasing plate area makes the capacitance ___, and at fixed voltage the stored energy ½CV² therefore ___.

choices.label: D
choices.text: stay the same; stay the same

hints.rationale: C ∝ A, so both capacitance and stored energy grow.

hints.prompt: Because capacitance rises with area, which options (those saying capacitance 'decreases') can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The two options whose first blank is 'decreases'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two options whose first blank is 'increases'.

hints.rationale: Capacitance clearly increases with area, so both 'decreases' options are out.
